questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with acute onset of severe pain, photophobia, and blurred vision in her right eye. She reports that 30 minutes ago she accidentally splashed a household drain cleaner containing sodium hydroxide into her eye. On examination, conjunctival injection is noted, and the cornea appears hazy with an epithelial defect. The anterior chamber is quiet. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Household drain cleaners often contain strong alkalis like sodium hydroxide. Alkali burns cause rapid penetration, epithelial loss, and stromal haze. Acid burns typically produce coagulative necrosis with a clearer demarcation. Corneal abrasion would have no chemical history. Conjunctivitis lacks epithelial defect and haze. Acute glaucoma presents with elevated IOP and a shallow anterior chamber.

questions.stem: A 42-year-old male construction worker presents to the eye clinic with a two-hour history of mild discomfort and redness in his left eye. He was mixing cement and felt something splash into his eye, which he washed out with water immediately. On examination, there is conjunctival injection and a small area of corneal haze inferiorly, with no epithelial defect. The pH of the tear film is 8.0. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Cement contains calcium hydroxide, a strong alkali. The elevated tear pH confirms ongoing alkali exposure despite irrigation. Dry eye, allergy, foreign body, and herpetic keratitis would not cause a tear pH of 8.0.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department after a domestic accident involving an unidentified cleaning product sprayed into both eyes. She has severe pain and near-complete vision loss in the left eye. On examination, the left cornea is opaque, and there is a white, avascular appearance to the limbus (limbal ischaemia) for more than 270 degrees. The right eye shows conjunctival injection and mild corneal haze inferiorly. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Limbal ischaemia involving greater than half the circumference is characteristic of severe alkali burns, which penetrate deeply and cause limbal stem cell damage. Acid burns typically cause less limbal ischaemia due to coagulative necrosis that limits penetration. Thermal burns would not cause the pH changes or bilateral presentation. Corneal perforation would have a flat chamber or visible leak. Acute glaucoma would have elevated IOP, not corneal opacity and limbal ischaemia.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman presented to the emergency department three days ago with a mild alkali burn to her right eye, which was treated conservatively with irrigation and lubricants. She returns with increasing pain, photophobia, and mucopurulent discharge. On examination, there is a corneal ulcer with a dense white infiltrate and an anterior chamber hypopyon of 1 mm. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: A new infiltrate and hypopyon three days after a chemical injury, especially with worsening pain and purulent discharge, suggests secondary bacterial infection. Sterile hypopyon from inflammation would be associated with a quiet eye and no infiltrate. Fungal keratitis often has a more indolent course and feathery margins. Herpetic keratitis would have dendritic ulcers. Corneal melting would present with thinning without purulent discharge.

questions.stem: A 6-year-old boy is brought to the paediatric emergency department by his mother, who says he sprayed a 'laundry detergent pod' into his eyes about an hour ago. He is crying and rubbing his eyes but does not report severe pain. On examination, he has bilateral conjunctival injection, mild corneal epithelial irregularity, and the tear pH is 7.8. The mother states he flushed his eyes with water at home for a few seconds. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Laundry detergent pods contain alkaline surfactants and can cause significant ocular surface injury, even if the pain is not immediately severe. The elevated tear pH and epithelial irregularity indicate an ongoing chemical burn. Allergic conjunctivitis would have itching and not affect pH. Viral conjunctivitis would have a watery discharge and preauricular lymphadenopathy. Corneal abrasion would have a clear history of trauma. Foreign body in both eyes is unlikely.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old builder presents to the emergency department 30 minutes after cement powder blew into his eyes. He complains of severe pain and photophobia. Visual acuity is 6/18 in the right eye and 6/12 in the left. On examination, there is conjunctival injection and mild corneal haze. Which single investigation should be performed first to confirm the diagnosis?
options: Slit lamp examination with fluorescein staining
questions.explanation: A strong alkali injury (cement produces calcium hydroxide) is suspected. Immediate pH testing is essential to confirm ocular surface alkalinity and guide continued irrigation. Slit lamp with fluorescein is important but should follow pH measurement.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old mechanic presents after a splash of battery acid (sulphuric acid) to his left eye. He immediately washed his eye with tap water for 5 minutes. On arrival, he has mild discomfort and conjunctival injection. pH is 7.2. Which investigation is most appropriate to assess the extent of ocular injury?
options: Slit lamp examination with fluorescein staining
questions.explanation: Although pH has normalized, fluorescein staining will reveal any corneal epithelial defect or conjunctival chemosis, confirming the diagnosis of chemical keratopathy. Repeat pH is not needed as it is normal.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman sustains an ammonia injury to both eyes at work. On examination, there is conjunctival blanching and chemosis. Slit lamp shows a hazy cornea but no epithelial defect. Which finding on examination is most useful for grading the severity of the injury?
options: Size of conjunctival epithelial defect on fluorescein
Depth of corneal haze graded on a scale of 1-4
Anterior chamber depth on slit lamp
Percentage of limbal ischaemia on slit lamp
questions.explanation: In chemical burns, the degree of limbal ischaemia (measured in clock hours) is a key determinant of severity and prognosis in the Roper-Hall classification. IOP may be elevated but is not a primary grading criterion.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man presents with a red painful eye 2 days after a chemical splash. He now has increased intraocular pressure (32 mmHg) and the anterior chamber appears shallow on slit lamp. Which investigation is most appropriate to assess the mechanism of raised IOP?
questions.explanation: Chemical injury can cause angle closure due to inflammation or swelling. Gonioscopy directly visualizes the angle to determine if it is open or closed, guiding management. Ultrasound biomicroscopy is an alternative but gonioscopy is the standard first-line.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old contact lens wearer presents with bilateral eye pain, photophobia, and redness after accidentally using a hydrogen peroxide cleaning solution without neutralizing it. She has washed her eyes at home. Visual acuity is 6/9. Slit lamp examination is being performed. Which fluorescein staining pattern is most characteristic of this chemical injury?
options: Central epithelial defect with stromal ring
Superficial punctate keratitis in a whorl pattern
questions.explanation: Hydrogen peroxide toxicity typically causes diffuse punctate epithelial erosions, often with an inferior predominance due to pooling of the solution in the inferior fornix. Dendritic ulcers suggest herpes simplex, and a central defect with ring suggests fungal keratitis.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old mechanic presents 2 hours after a splash of sodium hydroxide to his left eye. He immediately irrigated for 15 minutes at work. On exam, visual acuity 6/9, conjunctival injection, clear cornea, no epithelial defect. What is the most appropriate first-line therapy?
options: Topical prednisolone acetate 1% four times daily
questions.explanation: In mild chemical injury without epithelial defect, topical corticosteroids reduce inflammation and scarring; antibiotic prophylaxis is also given but the defining therapy is the steroid.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old presents with white-eye appearance after ammonia splash, limbal ischemia >180°, and corneal opacity. After immediate irrigation, what is the most appropriate first-line medical therapy?
questions.explanation: In severe alkali burns with limbal ischemia, topical ascorbate promotes collagen synthesis and reduces ulceration; amniotic membrane is considered for later reconstruction.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old lab technician splashes hydrofluoric acid in his right eye. After immediate irrigation, he still has intense pain and corneal oedema. What additional therapy is most appropriate?
questions.explanation: Hydrofluoric acid penetrates deeply and requires calcium or magnesium to bind fluoride ions; topical calcium gluconate drops are first-line after irrigation.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old developed secondary glaucoma 4 weeks after a severe alkali burn. Gonioscopy shows angle closure from peripheral anterior synechiae. Which first-line therapy is most appropriate to lower intraocular pressure?
questions.explanation: In inflammatory glaucoma, prostaglandin analogues may worsen inflammation; topical beta-blockers like timolol are first-line agents to lower intraocular pressure.

questions.stem: A 24-year-old with a moderate alkali burn now has conjunctivalisation of the cornea and early symblepharon formation at week 3. What is the most appropriate treatment to prevent further symblepharon?
questions.explanation: Mechanical separation with a symblepharon ring is first-line to prevent adhesion; amniotic membrane grafting is reserved for more extensive conjunctival scarring.

questions.stem: A 34-year-old construction worker presents to ED immediately after splashing an unknown liquid into his right eye at work. He is in significant pain, unable to open his eye. He has a bottle of the liquid labeled 'concrete cleaner' (strong alkali). On examination, conjunctival injection and corneal haze are noted. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Instill fluorescein and examine under blue light
questions.explanation: The priority in chemical eye injury is immediate copious irrigation to remove the chemical. Even before full assessment, irrigation should be started. Sweeping fornices can be done during irrigation but is not the first step.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman splashed a small amount of household bleach into her left eye 30 minutes ago. She rinsed her eye with water for 5 minutes at home. In ED, she has mild conjunctival injection, clear cornea, and visual acuity 6/6. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Measure pH of the conjunctival fornix
questions.explanation: After initial irrigation, pH should be checked to confirm adequate removal of chemical. If pH is still abnormal, further irrigation is needed. Other steps like fluorescein or medication come after pH is confirmed neutral.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man presents 1 hour after a splash of concentrated ammonia into his right eye. He has severe pain, photophobia, and blurred vision. Slit-lamp exam shows complete corneal epithelial loss, stromal haze, and 50% limbal ischemia. After copious irrigation for 30 minutes, the pH is neutral. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
questions.explanation: After irrigation and pH neutralization, the next priority is to assess for secondary complications, particularly elevated IOP, which is common in severe alkali burns. Cycloplegic and steroid are important but not immediately before assessing IOP.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old woman with a severe alkali burn 4 days ago is on topical dexamethasone, chloramphenicol, and atropine. She reports progressive pain and headache. IOP is 38 mmHg in the affected eye. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Urgent ophthalmology review for glaucoma surgery
questions.explanation: In acute IOP elevation after chemical injury, oral acetazolamide is used when topical agents are insufficient. It reduces aqueous production and lowers IOP rapidly. Topical adjuncts may be tried, but oral acetazolamide is the next step for urgent control.

questions.stem: A 19-year-old contact lens wearer inadvertently uses a hydrogen peroxide-based cleaning solution directly into his left eye. He removes the lens immediately and presents to ED within minutes. His eye is red and painful, but visual acuity is 6/9. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Irrigate the eye with normal saline
Examine with slit lamp and fluorescein
Remove any remaining contact lens fragments
questions.explanation: Although the contact lens was already removed, the chemical remains on the ocular surface. Immediate irrigation is essential to remove any residual hydrogen peroxide. pH check and slit-lamp exam can follow after irrigation.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man splashes bleach into his right eye. He complains of pain and blurred vision. On examination, you note conjunctival injection and mild corneal haze. What single finding on slit-lamp examination is most indicative of a severe chemical injury?
questions.explanation: Limbal ischaemia indicates damage to the limbal stem cell niche, a key prognostic factor for severe injury. Other findings are less specific for severity.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman attends the emergency department after a splash of paint stripper to her face. She has no previous ocular history. Eye irrigation was started en route. On arrival, her conjunctiva is injected and there is a small epithelial defect. Her visual acuity is 6/9. What single finding indicates that immediate further irrigation and urgent ophthalmology consultation are required?
options: A retained foreign body under the eyelid
pH of 8.0 after 1 litre of irrigation
questions.explanation: A pH >7.7 after irrigation indicates persistent alkalinity and ongoing chemical exposure, requiring further irrigation and specialist review.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old construction worker is brought in with bilateral severe alkali injury from cement. He has complete corneal opacification in both eyes. You note that his intraocular pressure is 55 mmHg in the right eye. What is the most likely life-threatening complication that must be excluded?
questions.explanation: Severe alkali burns cause corneal collagenolysis and thinning. Very high IOP can precipitate corneal perforation, a sight-threatening emergency. Other options are less directly associated.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman presents 2 days after a splash of ammonia to her left eye. She was discharged from the emergency department the day before after irrigation. She now has increasing pain and photophobia. On examination, you see a corneal epithelial defect with a white infiltrate at the edge. What single finding on examination would indicate that she has developed microbial keratitis, a sight-threatening complication?
questions.explanation: In the setting of a corneal infiltrate after chemical injury, hypopyon indicates intraocular infection, confirming microbial keratitis—a surgical emergency.

questions.stem: A 3-year-old child is brought to the emergency department after getting hand soap in their eyes. The mother has already rinsed the eyes with water. The child is crying but can open the eyes. On examination, the conjunctiva is slightly injected, and fluorescein shows no uptake. What single finding would mandate immediate referral to ophthalmology?
options: Visual acuity of 6/12 using Kay pictures
questions.explanation: A pH >7.7 after irrigation indicates retained alkaline chemical, requiring further irrigation and urgent ophthalmology assessment to prevent corneal damage.
